steps.title: Identify the dash-framed list structure
steps.content: The sentence uses a dash after territories to introduce a detailed list of three items: New Granada (present-day Colombia and Panama), Venezuela, and Quito (present-day [BLANK]). After this list, the phrase from colonial rule describes what the territories were liberated from. The blank must close the opening parenthesis (present-day and provide proper punctuation to mark the boundary between the end of the list and the following modifier.

steps.title: Match the dash-separated pattern
steps.content:
The structure requires closing the parenthesis and using a dash to separate the detailed list from from colonial rule. This creates the pattern — [detailed list] — [modifier], which is the standard convention for framing a detailed list before returning to the main sentence flow.

Option D provides Ecuador)—, which includes both the closing parenthesis and the dash needed to mark the transition. The other options fail: Option A places a comma incorrectly inside the parenthesis; Option B omits the boundary dash entirely; Option C uses a comma instead of the dash that properly sets apart the modifier. The opening and closing dashes create a clear, parallel structure that guides the reader from the listed territories to their shared modifier.

That matches choice D — D is correct.
